summary: This post serves as a table of contents for Joel Spolsky's planned series on 'Great Design,' organized into three sections: Introduction, Good Design, and Great Design. Many articles are derived from his book 'User Interface Design for Programmers.' The series covers fundamentals like user models, affordances, and usability testing, then advances to deeper topics like emotional design, social interfaces, and the tension between user happiness and user success. Most entries are listed but not yet written, making this more of an outline than a finished work.
key_points: The series is structured as a progression from basic design principles ('Good Design') to more nuanced, harder-to-define qualities ('Great Design')
Good Design topics focus on practical UI concerns: user models, affordances, modes, usability testing, and designing for user limitations
Great Design topics venture into aesthetics, emotional design, community building, and knowing when to remove features rather than add them
The framework distinguishes between user happiness and user success as potentially competing goals
money_quotes: Articles in gray are still being written.
key_insight: Great design goes beyond usable interfaces into emotional resonance, community dynamics, and the discipline of removing features — a progression from competence to artistry.
